market_summary.narrative: U.S. equities suffered a fourth consecutive week of declines, with major indexes falling 1.9–2.1% to their lowest levels since September 2025. The primary driver was escalating geopolitical risk from the U.S.-Iran conflict, now in its fourth week, which has sent Brent crude surging ~8.8% on the week and above $114/barrel as strikes hit Middle Eastern energy infrastructure including Qatar's LNG export facility. Hotter-than-expected inflation data compounded the selloff — February PPI rose 0.7% MoM against a 0.3% consensus estimate — while the Fed held rates steady at 3.50–3.75% on March 18 and revised its 2026 inflation forecast up to 2.7%. The selloff was broad-based across all eleven S&P sectors except Energy (+3%) and Financials (flat), with Utilities (-5%), Consumer Staples (-4%), and Real Estate (-4%) leading declines as Treasury yields climbed on persistent inflation fears. Notably, the VIX actually eased 1.5% despite the equity drawdown, suggesting orderly de-risking rather than panic selling; the S&P 500 also broke decisively below its 200-day moving average. Monday morning (Mar 23) futures rallied ~1.5–2% after Trump's Truth Social post suggested he is considering 'winding down' the Iran conflict, boosting risk sentiment.

value_picks.name: Novo Nordisk A/S
value_picks.rev_growth: +2.3% (DKK FY2025 vs FY2024; ~+11% USD-adjusted per company reporting)
value_picks.analyst_consensus: Hold (MarketBeat: 4 Buy, 18 Hold, 1 Sell; avg target $65.56 representing ~79% upside)
value_picks.why_undervalued: NVO has shed ~73% from its June 2024 all-time high of $142, driven by the February 2026 Phase 3 miss of CagriSema (a dual semaglutide+cagrilintide combo) and management guidance for -5% to -13% organic sales growth in 2026 as Wegovy list prices are set to be halved in the US by 2027. Markets have extrapolated short-term pipeline disappointment into permanent impairment, compressing valuation to a historic trough P/E of ~10.4x for what remains a $47B revenue, 50%-EBITDA-margin global pharmaceutical franchise with leading market share in a structurally growing GLP-1 category.
value_picks.bull_case: Ozempic and Wegovy together remain the gold-standard GLP-1 treatments in a market where demand vastly exceeds supply, and the company retains a broad pipeline (oral semaglutide, amycretin, CagriSema further studies) plus expanding cardiac, MASH, and CKD indications. With average analyst price targets of $65.56 implying 79% upside and a simple fair-value DCF pointing toward $95 per share according to Simply Wall St analysis, the stock's P/E of ~10x prices in near-complete franchise failure — a scenario inconsistent with 11% recent revenue growth and $8B+ annual free cash flow. Berenberg initiated coverage with a $175 price target as recently as March 2026.

value_picks.name: The Kraft Heinz Company
value_picks.pe_ratio: N/M (GAAP; adj. fwd P/E ~10.5x based on guided $1.98-$2.10 adj. EPS)
value_picks.analyst_consensus: Hold (11 analysts: 0 Buy, 9 Hold, 2 Sell; avg target $25.18 representing ~17% upside from current price)
value_picks.why_undervalued: KHC is trading within 0.3% of its 52-week low, pressed down by $9.3B in non-cash goodwill impairment charges in FY2025 and the CEO's February 2026 pivot that halted the planned company separation and announced a $600M multi-year reinvestment plan, generating acute uncertainty. GAAP EPS turned deeply negative (-$4.91), masking remarkably robust cash generation: FCF of $3.66B grew 15.9% YoY, implying a 14.4% FCF yield on a ~$25B market cap — a level rarely seen in investment-grade large-cap consumer staples.
value_picks.bull_case: The non-cash impairments are accounting fiction that do not impair Kraft Heinz's ability to generate cash; FCF conversion is guided to ~100% in FY2026, and the company returned $2.3B to shareholders (dividends + buybacks) in FY2025. With iconic brands including Heinz, Kraft, Oscar Mayer, Philadelphia, and Lunchables, and the separation now shelved in favor of a focused $600M investment in brand-building and product superiority, management has clarity of mission. At 10.5x adjusted earnings and 9.4x adjusted EBITDA, the stock prices in minimal recovery — any stabilization of organic sales from -3.5% to flat would be a substantial positive catalyst.

value_picks.name: Unilever PLC
value_picks.rev_growth: -17.0% (FY2025 vs FY2024 on reported basis; reflects completed divestiture of ice cream/Magnum/Ben & Jerry's division; underlying organic growth positive)
value_picks.analyst_consensus: Moderate Buy (MarketBeat/Yahoo Finance consensus: avg target ~$76-$77 representing ~24-25% upside from current price)
value_picks.why_undervalued: Unilever's ADR is within 0.4% of its 52-week low at $61.48, weighed down by broader consumer staples sector de-rating and near-term margin pressure from commodity costs. The reported revenue decline from €60.8B to €50.5B in FY2025 is almost entirely attributable to the sale of the ice cream segment (Magnum, Ben & Jerry's, Wall's) for approximately €15B in proceeds — underlying organic growth and profitability in the retained portfolio (Beauty & Wellbeing, Personal Care, Home Care, Nutrition) is accelerating under CEO Hein Schumacher's 'Growth Action Plan.'
value_picks.bull_case: With the €15B ice cream disposal proceeds now available for deleveraging and shareholder returns, Unilever's balance sheet trajectory improves materially in 2026. The retained portfolio commands higher margins and better growth profiles than the divested ice cream business; Power Brands (representing 75%+ of turnover) grew mid-single digits in 2025. Trading at 16.2x EV/EBITDA versus peer Procter & Gamble at ~20x, with analysts setting avg 12-month targets of $76-$77 (+24-25%), the stock offers a compelling entry at a structural discount to best-in-class FMCG peers during a period of transformational portfolio improvement.

value_picks.name: Mondelez International, Inc.
value_picks.rev_growth: +5.8% (FY2025 $38.54B vs FY2024 $36.44B, includes acquisitions; organic growth ~flat due to cocoa cost-driven pricing dynamics)
value_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy (12 analysts: 9 Bullish/75%, 3 Neutral/25%, 0 Bearish; avg target $66.17 representing ~17.7% upside)
value_picks.why_undervalued: Mondelez is trading 21% off its 52-week high and within 9.8% of its 52-week low as the market penalizes the company for a cyclical surge in cocoa and sugar input costs that are compressing near-term gross margins by ~420 basis points in H1 2026. GAAP net income halved in FY2025 due to mark-to-market commodity hedging losses and elevated raw material costs, but this obscures the durability of the underlying business: Mondelez commands #1 or #2 positions in chocolate (Cadbury, Milka, Toblerone), biscuits (Oreo, Chips Ahoy), and gum/candy in over 150 countries.
value_picks.bull_case: Cocoa costs are commodity inputs that revert cyclically; futures pricing as of Q1 2026 indicates declining cocoa costs in 2H 2026 and into 2027, which would mechanically restore Mondelez's gross margins to normalized levels and drive substantial EPS recovery. With 75% of covering analysts rating the stock a Buy or Strong Buy and average 12-month price targets of $66.17 (+17.7%), the Street is uniformly bullish on a mean-reversion thesis. Morgan Stanley raised its target to $70 in March 2026 citing the company's resilient volume share gains and the underappreciated contribution of emerging-market growth (Developing Markets contributed over 35% of revenue).

value_picks.name: Constellation Brands, Inc.
value_picks.rev_growth: +2.5% (FY2025 $10.21B vs FY2024 $9.96B; Beer segment organic sales recently under pressure from tariff/consumer headwinds)
value_picks.analyst_consensus: Buy (11 analysts: 6 Bullish/54.5%, 5 Neutral/45.5%, 0 Bearish; avg target $163.91 representing ~9.5% upside; Bernstein maintains Outperform at $197; Citi upgraded to Buy at $175 in March 2026)
value_picks.why_undervalued: Constellation Brands has declined ~24% from its 52-week high and sits near a multi-year valuation trough at 12x forward earnings, driven by a cyclical volume softening in the beer segment (U.S. consumers pulling back on discretionary spending), tariff risk on Mexican beer imports, and the write-down of the wine/spirits business (divested in 2024-2025). The market has conflated temporary volume softness with structural impairment of Constellation's beer brands — but Modelo Especial remained the #1 best-selling beer by dollar sales in the U.S. for Q3 2025 and Pacifico and Corona continue to gain shelf space.
value_picks.bull_case: Constellation's beer portfolio is among the most competitively defensible consumer brands in America, with imported Mexican beer the only major beer segment in the U.S. growing market share over 5+ years. The company generated $1.76B in free cash flow in its most recent fiscal year and has a $4B share buyback authorization, supporting aggressive capital return. Citigroup upgraded STZ to Buy with a $175 target in March 2026, noting that the risk/reward is highly compelling at 12x forward earnings with volume recovery expected in H2 2026 as consumer confidence stabilizes. The divestiture of the wine/spirits segment simplifies the story to a pure-play premium beer company with 40%+ EBITDA margins.

momentum_picks.name: GE Vernova Inc.
momentum_picks.rev_growth: +8.9% YoY (FY2025 $38.1B vs FY2024 $34.9B); net income up 215% YoY
momentum_picks.catalyst: GE Vernova is the purest large-cap play on AI-driven electricity demand and grid infrastructure buildout. On its Q4 2025 earnings call (January 28, 2026), management raised full-year 2026 revenue guidance to $44–$45B, lifted free cash flow guidance to $5–$5.5B, doubled the dividend, and expanded share buyback authorization to $10B. The company entered 2026 with a $150B backlog—including data-center-related Electrification orders exceeding $2B in 2025 (3x the 2024 level)—and recently announced a $200M transformer facility in Vietnam and a $30M Italy plant expansion, extending manufacturing capacity for HVDC grid infrastructure. In March 2026, Rothschild upgraded GEV from Sell to Buy with a $1,100 target, the highest on the Street, citing the backlog's embedded margin trajectory.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The bull case rests on GEV's $64B equipment backlog carrying 6 additional margin points already contracted, which makes the current ~48x trailing P/E look deceptively expensive relative to forward earnings power—consensus projects EBITDA margins expanding from 12.9% in 2025 to 17.1% in 2026 and 20.4% by 2028. The Street average price target of $884.80 implies roughly 4% upside from current levels, with the high target of $1,100 (Rothschild) representing 29% upside for those who believe the electrification supercycle is still in early innings. Key risks include slippage in transformer manufacturing capacity ramps, any deceleration in hyperscaler capex, and execution on the Prolec GE acquisition integration; the stock has already run ~168% over 12 months, leaving little margin for execution misses.
momentum_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy | Avg PT $884.80 | High $1,100 | 4 Bullish / 1 Neutral

momentum_picks.name: Seagate Technology Holdings plc
momentum_picks.rev_growth: +38.9% YoY (FY2025 ended June 2025: $9.1B vs $6.6B); EPS up 333% YoY
momentum_picks.catalyst: Seagate is the defining beneficiary of what analysts are calling the AI 'Storage Supercycle.' Its proprietary HAMR-based Mozaic 3+ platform—currently shipping 30TB+ drives—gives it a structural capacity density advantage over competitors, enabling higher TB-per-watt efficiency that hyperscalers require for AI data lakes. On its January 28, 2026 fiscal Q2 earnings, Seagate reported $2.82B revenue (+21.5% YoY) and EPS of $3.11 (+53%), and disclosed it has already sold out anticipated production of high-capacity nearline drives through 2026 with orders extending into H1 2027. Management projects HDD market revenue to grow at mid-teens CAGR from 2024–2028, with Seagate's addressable opportunity potentially doubling to $23B by 2028 as generative video AI requires exponentially larger cold-storage capacity.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The reward thesis centers on Seagate's transition from cyclical commodity supplier to a contracted infrastructure utility—Build-to-Order contracts with major cloud customers provide revenue visibility into 2028, which was entirely absent in prior upcycles. Analyst consensus is 100% bullish with a $527.50 average price target (Cantor Fitzgerald highest at $650), implying 28–58% upside from current levels. The primary risks are valuation (stock is up ~392% over 12 months), potential upside surprise already priced into HDD pricing assumptions, and the structural long-term threat of declining SSD costs compressing HDD's cost-per-TB advantage over a 5–7 year horizon.
momentum_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy | Avg PT $527.50 | High $650 | 4 Bullish / 0 Neutral

momentum_picks.name: Teradyne, Inc.
momentum_picks.rev_growth: +13.1% YoY (FY2025 $3.19B vs FY2024 $2.82B); strong inflection point ahead
momentum_picks.catalyst: Teradyne is the dominant supplier of automated test equipment (ATE) for AI semiconductors, with AI-driven compute revenue crossing 60% of total revenue in Q4 2025 and guided above 70% in Q1 2026. The stock surged 23.7% on February 3, 2026, when management guided Q1 2026 revenue to a record $1.2B midpoint—a 75% year-over-year jump—anchored by qualification progress on a major merchant GPU customer's production line expected to generate initial production revenue in H2 2026. ARK Invest holds over $456M in TER (Top 10 position) and Goldman Sachs raised its target to $300 (from $230) post-earnings. The e-commerce robotics segment via Universal Robots is guided to triple in FY2026, while a new U.S. operations hub in Wixom, Michigan supports domestic manufacturing re-shoring demand.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The FY2026 consensus EPS estimate of ~$6.24 represents a 57.7% jump versus FY2025 actuals, yet TER trades at only ~47x trailing earnings—reasonable if the GPU ATE ramp proceeds on schedule. All 5 most recent analyst ratings are bullish, with targets clustering $300–$330 (avg $320), implying ~10% upside from the current price. The 1-month pullback of -10.5% from the post-earnings highs represents a potential entry opportunity ahead of the H2 2026 merchant GPU production revenue catalyst. Key risks include delays in GPU production qualification timelines, cyclical exposure to semiconductor capex spending patterns, and whether the robotics segment ramp is commercially real—prior robotics cycles have disappointed.
momentum_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy | Avg PT $320 | High $330 | 5 Bullish / 0 Neutral

momentum_picks.name: Comfort Systems USA, Inc.
momentum_picks.rev_growth: +29.5% YoY (FY2025 $9.1B vs FY2024 $7.0B); net income up 96% YoY
momentum_picks.catalyst: Comfort Systems is the nation's leading mechanical-electrical-plumbing (MEP) contractor and has structurally re-rated from a cyclical construction company to a data center infrastructure platform. Its project backlog hit $11.9B at year-end 2025—nearly double the year-ago level—driven almost entirely by AI and cloud data center construction mandates from hyperscalers. In Q4 2025, Comfort Systems achieved a historic 25.5% gross margin, breaking a 25% threshold for the first time in company history. Management is expanding modular/prefabrication capacity by 30% in 2026 (from 3M to 4M sq ft) to handle the specialized HVAC and cooling systems demanded by high-density AI computing facilities. Consensus projects FY2026 revenue of $10.95B (+20.3% YoY) and EPS of $36.76 (+27.3%).
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The near-doubling of backlog provides exceptional near-term earnings visibility—hyperscaler capex programs are multi-year commitments—and the company's proprietary modular construction methods provide a durable competitive moat against smaller contractors. DA Davidson maintains a $1,800 target (vs. $1,357 current), implying ~33% upside. The 1-month pullback of -7.2% from all-time highs offers a technically constructive entry. Primary risks are labor cost inflation in a tight construction labor market, insider selling following the large price appreciation, and the stock's elevated P/E of ~47x vs. industry average ~33x which requires continued execution above consensus—any backlog conversion slowdown could be punished severely.
momentum_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy | Avg PT $1,378.67 | High $1,800 | 3 Bullish / 0 Neutral

momentum_picks.name: Corning Incorporated
momentum_picks.rev_growth: +19.1% YoY (FY2025 $15.6B vs FY2024 $13.1B); net income up 215% YoY
momentum_picks.catalyst: Corning is a 175-year-old specialty glass manufacturer that has reinvented itself as the backbone of AI optical infrastructure. Its Optical Communications segment grew 35% in FY2025 to $6.3B, with enterprise data center revenue surging 61%. The pivotal re-rating catalyst was a $6B multi-year fiber supply contract with Meta (announced 2025) plus a $2.5B ongoing commitment with Apple for cover glass, which together provided unprecedented revenue visibility. Bank of America projects Corning's scale-out revenue opportunity will reach $10.3B by 2030, and Citi set a Street-high $170 target in late February 2026. Management's enhanced 'Springboard' strategy targets $6.5B in additional annual sales by end-2026, with a 20%+ core operating margin already achieved in Q4 2025.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The -10.6% pullback from the February 2026 peak near $162 offers a materially more attractive entry than the all-time high, with the stock now trading 23% below Citi's $170 target and 15% below UBS's $160 target. The bull case is a structural, multi-year shift in optical fiber demand driven by AI infrastructure rather than a cyclical capex peak—the key question analysts debate. Corning's 68x trailing P/E reflects this growth premium and represents the primary risk: if hyperscaler spending decelerates or the Meta contract ramps more slowly than expected, the valuation is vulnerable. The 25%+ pullback from the high and 75% bullish analyst consensus (avg PT $150.25, implying +20% upside) argue for asymmetric reward at current levels.
momentum_picks.analyst_consensus: Strong Buy | Avg PT $150.25 | High $170 | 3 Bullish / 1 Neutral

trends.name: Iran War Energy Shock & Inflation Re-acceleration
trends.description: The U.S.-Israel military campaign against Iran, now in its fourth week, has disrupted global energy supply chains and sent oil prices soaring more than 48% since the conflict began, with Brent crude topping $114/barrel after strikes on Qatar's LNG export hub and Iran's South Pars gas field. Rising energy costs are pushing inflation expectations sharply higher — February PPI came in at +0.7% MoM vs. +0.3% expected, and the Fed raised its 2026 PCE inflation forecast to 2.7%. Markets now price just one Fed rate cut for all of 2026, with some participants pricing mild tightening, effectively eliminating the monetary easing tailwind that underpinned equities in late 2025.

trends.name: AI Infrastructure Hyperscaler Capex Supercycle
trends.description: Big Tech is committing unprecedented capital to AI infrastructure: Amazon ($200B in 2026 capex, +52% YoY), Alphabet ($175–185B, doubling for second consecutive year), Meta ($115–135B), and Microsoft (~$97–100B) have collectively pledged ~$650B in 2026 spending. This is driving a memory shortage that has benefited Micron (MU) — which reported Q2 fiscal 2026 revenue of $23.86B (+196% YoY, beating estimates by $4.96B) — and Sandisk (SNDK), while lifting networking plays like Arista, Marvell, and Broadcom. The memory shortage is concurrently pressuring PC and smartphone demand, with IDC forecasting 11–13% unit shipment declines in 2026.

trends.name: 'SaaSpocalypse' — AI Disruption of the Software Seat Model
trends.description: Enterprise software is facing a fundamental reckoning as AI agents threaten the per-seat licensing model that has underpinned SaaS valuations for two decades. Investors have wiped over $1 trillion in market cap from the sector in early 2026, with the iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) down roughly 25% YTD. Pure-play per-seat pricing adoption has fallen from 21% to 15% of enterprises, with 70% now demanding usage-based or outcome-based contracts. Adobe (ADBE, down ~30% from 52-week high), Salesforce (CRM, down ~26% YTD), and Atlassian (TEAM) have been hardest hit, though ServiceNow (NOW) has emerged as a relative resilient play with its $600M+ 'Now Assist' ACV.

trends.name: Defense AI & Geopolitical Technology Spending
trends.description: The Iran war has catalyzed renewed government spending on AI-enabled defense and intelligence technologies. Palantir (PLTR) is emerging as a key beneficiary, with its U.S. government revenue up 66% YoY in Q4 2025 (40% of total revenue), defense contract wins including a $10B U.S. Army framework, and Rosenblatt raising its price target to $200. Cybersecurity demand is also elevated as the war raises nation-state threat concerns, with cyber ETFs gaining traction and PANW's Q2 NGS ARR up 33% YoY. BWX Technologies (BWXT), a nuclear components supplier to the U.S. government, has gained ~18% over three months and was upgraded to a Zacks Strong Buy on March 20.

trends.name: Memory & Storage as Strategic AI Infrastructure Assets
trends.description: High-bandwidth memory (HBM) and NAND flash storage have been repriced as strategic assets in the AI arms race, with SK Hynix's chairman projecting the memory shortage will persist four to five years. Sandisk (SNDK) is up ~1,271% since its February 2025 spin-off from Western Digital, and Micron's blowout Q2 earnings (Q3 revenue guidance of $33.5B vs. $23.7B expected) confirmed the cycle is accelerating. DRAM pricing is expected to rise 80–85% this quarter per TrendForce data, benefiting the entire memory supply chain while creating cost headwinds for PC OEMs, smartphone makers, and consumer electronics.

risks.name: Iran War Escalation & Strait of Hormuz Closure
risks.description: The ongoing U.S.-Israel military campaign against Iran has already caused the largest disruption in oil supply history per the IEA, with Brent crude up ~48% since the conflict began and Qatar declaring force majeure on LNG exports. A deeper escalation — particularly a sustained closure of the Strait of Hormuz — could push oil beyond $150/barrel and trigger a stagflationary shock that puts global central banks in an impossible position between fighting inflation and supporting growth. The U.S. has reportedly considered deploying ground troops and taking over Iran's Kharg Island oil export terminal, which could provoke a broader regional conflict involving Gulf producers.

risks.name: Persistent Inflation Forcing Fed Rate Hike
risks.description: The Fed held rates at 3.50–3.75% on March 18 while raising its 2026 inflation forecast to 2.7%, but markets have moved aggressively from pricing ~20bps of easing to pricing ~8bps of tightening by year-end — a historic inversion. If oil prices remain elevated and the Iran conflict persists, the Fed could be forced to hike rather than cut, representing the worst outcome for equity markets. The implied probability of a rate hike at the April meeting has already risen from near-zero to 6.2%, and rate cut expectations have been pushed to autumn 2027 at the earliest.

risks.name: AI Capex ROI Skepticism & Big Tech Multiple Compression
risks.description: Despite record AI infrastructure commitments ($650B combined for the four largest U.S. tech companies), investors are increasingly questioning when these investments will generate returns. Microsoft's stock fell 12% on its earnings day after disclosing that 45% of its cloud backlog depends on OpenAI, and Amazon is down ~15% since announcing its $200B capex plan. Only 14% of CFOs surveyed in March 2026 report measurable ROI from third-party AI investments. If hyperscaler earnings in upcoming quarters show margin compression without clear revenue acceleration from AI products, a broader de-rating of AI-adjacent valuations is likely.

risks.name: SaaS Revenue Model Disruption & Accelerated Seat Count Decline
risks.description: Anthropic's release of Claude Code Security — which autonomously scans codebases for vulnerabilities — triggered a sector-wide selloff in enterprise software in late February, and broader agentic AI tools are threatening the economic foundation of per-seat SaaS pricing. Atlassian reported its first-ever decline in enterprise seat counts in early 2026, and only 14% of CFOs report measurable AI ROI. The transition to outcome-based and usage-based pricing requires total overhauls of sales structures and creates near-term revenue recognition uncertainty for Salesforce, Adobe, ServiceNow, Workday, and others across a $1T+ sector.

risks.name: Memory Cycle Peak & Consumer Electronics Demand Destruction
risks.description: While the memory supercycle is currently in full swing, contrarian voices are emerging. Citron Research initiated a short position on Sandisk in late February citing a peaking memory cycle, and IDC has dramatically cut its 2026 PC and smartphone shipment forecasts to show 11–13% unit declines — partly attributable to memory price inflation. A faster-than-expected resolution of the Iran conflict that pulls oil and industrial prices lower could simultaneously reduce AI infrastructure spending urgency and trigger a sharp memory pricing downturn, potentially reversing the gains in MU, SNDK, and WDC that have been among the strongest performers YTD.

watchlist_movers.summary: NVDA held its GTC 2026 developers conference the week of March 16, unveiling the Vera Rubin GPU architecture, robotaxi partnerships (Uber, Lyft, WeRide), autonomous vehicle deals (BYD, Geely, Nissan, Isuzu), and NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion adoption by auto OEMs. Despite overwhelmingly positive news flow — including CEO Jensen Huang projecting $1T+ in Blackwell/Vera Rubin GPU orders through 2027 — the stock fell ~4% for the week as Iran war macro pressures offset AI optimism. NVDA reported record FY Q4 2026 revenue of $68.1B (+73% YoY) in late February with Q1 FY2027 guidance of $78B.

Broadcom closed lower on the week (~-3% Friday alone) as macro headwinds from the Iran war and inflation concerns weighed broadly on semiconductors. Q1 FY2026 results (reported early March) showed $4B in AI semiconductor revenue (+106% YoY) with Q2 guidance of $14.8B total and $8.2B AI segment revenue. The company projects $100B in AI chip revenue by end of 2027.

AMD was cited as a potential market share beneficiary as Nvidia concedes supply-constrained segments; Meta reportedly signed a multi-year AMD deal worth up to $60B for AI infrastructure. Stock under broad semiconductor sector pressure this week alongside Iran-war macro selloff.

Microsoft identified as the worst-performing Magnificent Seven stock in 2026, down ~3% on the week and flagged for slower Azure growth relative to peers and heavy dependence on OpenAI (45% of its $625B cloud backlog). Stock fell 12% on its January earnings day after disclosing OpenAI reliance; shares around $381 at week's end.

Amazon fell sharply earlier in 2026 (~-15% since announcing $200B 2026 capex plan, significantly above the ~$146B analyst expectation), but stabilized this week. AWS still commands 28% global cloud share despite gradual erosion. Amazon surpassed Walmart in 2025 to become the world's largest company by revenue at $716.9B.

Alphabet received multiple analyst upgrades: Wells Fargo upgraded to Overweight with $387 target, Tigress Financial raised target to $415 (Strong Buy), citing Gemini's leadership in AI search and cloud. Google Cloud revenue surged 48% YoY in Q4; Waymo now provides 400K rides/week with 21-city expansion planned for 2026.

Meta reported record Q4 2025 revenue of $59.9B (+24% YoY) with ad clicks up 3.5% and impressions up 18%. Pursuing stablecoin integration and signed a major AMD deal for AI infrastructure. Fell ~2% this week as Iran macro weighed on tech broadly.

Micron reported a blockbuster Q2 FY2026 on March 18: revenue of $23.86B (+196% YoY) beat estimates by $4.96B, with Q3 guidance of $33.5B vs. $23.7B expected — a stunning ~41% beat to consensus. Non-GAAP EPS guided to ~$19.15 vs. $11.29 consensus. Despite the blowout, shares fell ~5% in after-hours and ~3-5% on the week as elevated capital spending plans raised concerns and 'buy the rumor, sell the news' dynamics prevailed. Stock is up ~62% YTD and ~357% over 12 months.

Marvell Technology reported strong Q4 FY2026 earnings on March 5 (revenue $2.22B, +22% YoY; EPS $0.80 vs. $0.79 estimate) and issued Q1 FY2027 guidance of $2.40B (+~5% above consensus). FY2028 revenue guidance of ~$15B (vs. $12.92B expected) and FY2027 growth >30% drove the stock up ~18-23% on March 6. Multiple upgrades followed: BofA (Hold→Buy, $110 target), Benchmark (Hold→Buy, $130), Stifel ($120), Rosenblatt ($140). Stock is a key AI optical interconnect/custom silicon play.
watchlist_movers.weekly_change: +18.0% (week of Mar 6; estimated slight pullback in the current week)

watchlist_movers.summary: TSMC reported full-year 2025 revenue of $122.42B (+35.9% YoY) and guided 2026 revenue growth of ~30% ($158B consensus). AI-driven HPC now represents 58% of revenue. Stock is up ~34% over six months with a Zacks Strong Buy rating; trades at 23.5x forward P/E vs. 24.6x sector average.

ARM Holdings surged +5.19% on March 20 as Meta's choice of ARM-based CPUs for massive AI data centers alongside Nvidia GPUs highlighted ARM's expanding role in AI infrastructure. BofA revised its price target to $140, citing ARM's growing CPU market share in AI data centers and the Nvidia-MediaTek ARM-centric PC program. ARM CEO Rene Haas spoke of an 'explosion of demand' at an NVIDIA GTC panel.

Synopsys detailed integration of its $35B Ansys acquisition at a March 13 investor event, accelerating its shift from chip EDA to physical systems simulation. Q1 FY2026 revenue surged 65.5% YoY to $2.41B (includes Ansys). FY2026 guidance of $9.56–9.66B. Cadence and NVIDIA announced an 'Accelerated Engineering Solutions for Agentic AI Chip and System Design' partnership at GTC.

Cadence reported Q4 2025 earnings in February with revenue of $1.44B (+6% YoY), beating estimates; CDNS shares rose ~8% post-earnings. Consensus price target ~$377 (+27% implied upside). NVIDIA partnership for accelerated agentic AI chip design announced at GTC 2026.

CrowdStrike reported Q4 FY2026 on March 3: revenue $1.3B (+23% YoY), ending ARR of $5.25B — crossing the $5B milestone for the first time — with record $1.01B net new ARR. Q1 FY2027 revenue guidance of $1.36–1.364B in-line with consensus. CEO Kurtz called FY2026 'CrowdStrike's best year yet.' Stock is down ~13% YTD but received a Wedbush Buy with $600 price target (50% upside).

Palo Alto Networks reported Q2 FY2026 on February 17: revenue $2.6B (+15% YoY), EPS $1.03 vs. $0.94 expected; NGS ARR +33% YoY to $6.33B; third consecutive quarter of 30%+ non-GAAP operating margins. Closed the CyberArk ($25B) and Chronosphere acquisitions. Q3 FY2026 guidance of $2.941–2.945B revenue (+28–29%). Despite the beat, shares fell 5.12% in after-hours. Full-year FY2026 revenue guidance $11.28–11.31B (+22–23%). Wedbush maintains Buy with $225 target.

Zscaler received a Wedbush Buy reiterating with $350 price target (potential to double), calling it a 'premier name to own in the cyber space' with strong AI security positioning and robust product pipeline. ZS fell ~10%+ in the late February AI disruption selloff but has partially recovered.

Rubrik reported Q4 FY2026 on March 12–13: subscription ARR net retention >120%, strong guidance above expectations. Stock up ~11.9% in the month preceding the report. FedRAMP High authorization for federal XIoT/OT security achieved March 18, expanding public sector addressable market. CEO stated 'Rubrik cannot be vibe-coded' addressing AI disruption concerns.

Varonis (down ~40% YTD) drew a $128M institutional buy from Tremblant Capital Group on February 17, adding 3.05M shares. The stock's selloff is part of the broader 'SaaSpocalypse' in enterprise software; Tremblant's large purchase signals institutional contrarian conviction at depressed levels.

Salesforce reported Q4 FY2026 on February 25: revenue $11.2B (+12% YoY), EPS $3.81 vs. $3.05 expected (+24.9% beat); Agentforce and Data 360 ARR grew 200% YoY; 29,000+ Agentforce deals closed. FY2027 guidance: $45.8–46.2B revenue (+10–11%). Stock is down ~26–28% YTD despite the beat, reflecting 'SaaSpocalypse' fears; Wedbush reinstated as a Buy.

Adobe reported Q1 FY2026 on March 12: revenue $6.40B (+12% YoY, record Q1), non-GAAP EPS $6.06 vs. $5.46 expected (+11% beat); AI-first application ARR tripled YoY; monthly active users surpassed 850M (+17% YoY). Despite the beat, stock fell ~0.82% in after-hours. Full-year FY2026 ARR growth guidance of 10.2% maintained. Stock remains ~30% below 52-week high due to investor concerns about AI commoditizing creative tools.

ServiceNow highlighted as a 'rare resilient player' in the SaaSpocalypse, with Now Assist ACV at $600M+ and on track for $1B in 2026. Stock down ~25% YTD — a Benchmark analyst flagged it as 'oversold' and advisable to shift AI infrastructure capital toward NOW. Multiple analysts cite its workflow automation focus as more defensible than pure creative AI.

Workday reported Q4 FY2026 on February 27: revenue $2.532B (+14.5% YoY), subscription revenue $2.360B (+15.7%), non-GAAP EPS $2.47 vs. $1.92. Launched Sana AI platform, delivered 1.7B AI actions in FY2026. Q1 FY2027 subscription revenue guidance $2.3B slightly below consensus, pressuring stock. AI ACV doubled in most recent quarter.

Snowflake reported Q4 FY2026 earnings beat with revenue beating estimates by ~$50M, product revenue up 29% YoY to $1.16B, and RPO up 37% to $7.88B. Non-GAAP operating margin hit 10.5% (+400bps YoY). The $600M Observe acquisition bolsters its AI Data Cloud. BofA maintains Buy with $275 target; Bank of America sees SNOW as among the first data infra stocks to rebound from the SaaSpocalypse.

Palantir is up ~14% in March (partial recovery from ~16% YTD decline) as the Iran war boosts its defense AI narrative. U.S. government revenue grew 66% YoY in Q4 2025; 2026 revenue guidance of $7.19B (+61%, above prior consensus of $6.27B). Multiple upgrades: UBS Buy ($180 target), Daiwa Buy ($180), Rosenblatt Buy ($200). Stock at ~$150.68 as of March 21. Market cap ~$360B.

Datadog reported Q4 2025 results in February (revenue $953M, beat; EPS $0.59 vs. $0.55 expected), but FY2026 revenue guidance of $4.06–4.10B missed the $4.11B consensus, pressuring the stock. Goldman Sachs initiated with a Sell rating ($113 target) citing competitive pressures from Grafana, Clickhouse, Palo Alto/Chronosphere, and Amazon. Stock down ~38% over 3 months and ~21% over 12 months.

Arista Networks reported Q4 2025 (reported February 12): revenue $2.49B (+28.9% YoY), EPS $0.82 vs. $0.75 expected. CEO Jayshree Ullal guided 2026 revenue to $11B (vs. $9B in 2025, first time above $10B). TD Cowen initiated coverage Buy with $170 target (March 12). Consensus is Moderate Buy with $176.47 average target. Stock trades at ~$131 after recent declines from $165 52-week high.

Cloudflare trading around $215 as of March 23 (notably higher than analyst consensus targets of $125–180, reflecting premium AI/edge computing positioning). 'SaaSpocalypse' has hit Cloudflare: stock fell ~9.6% in late February AI scare selloff. No major material news this specific week beyond broad sector pressure.

Coinbase reported in talks to take a minority equity stake in Bybit (valued at ~$25B) as part of its 'Everything Exchange' strategy to enter offshore derivatives markets. Separately, Coinbase launched US stock/ETF trading for all U.S. users in late February 2026 and agentic wallets in early March. Crypto market broadly supported by Iran-war risk-on/off dynamics.

Hims & Hers surged ~57% the week of March 9 after announcing a landmark partnership with Novo Nordisk to sell Wegovy and Ozempic on its telehealth platform, ending their patent lawsuit. Novo dropped its infringement suit, and multiple analysts upgraded the stock: Citigroup ($24 target, Neutral from Sell), BofA ($23, Neutral from Underperform), Deutsche Bank ($28, Neutral). The deal adds a key GLP-1 revenue driver to Hims' 2.5M subscriber base. Stock remains volatile around $22–24.
watchlist_movers.weekly_change: +57.4% (week of Mar 9; partial reversal in subsequent week)

watchlist_movers.summary: SanDisk is up ~1,271% since its February 2025 spin-off from Western Digital, with Q2 FY2026 revenue near $3B (+61% YoY). Western Digital completed a $3.17B secondary offering of its remaining SNDK stake in February (discounted 7.7%), reducing its ownership to ~1.7M shares. Bernstein raised its target to $1,000 (Outperform), Cantor Fitzgerald to $800 (Overweight). Citron Research has initiated a short position. Stock was at $709.71 at week's end, down ~8% on the week.

Western Digital (rebranded as WD) is up ~472% since the Sandisk spin-off, benefiting from HDD demand for AI data centers. Completed the $3.17B secondary offering of remaining Sandisk shares in February. Currently closing in on $100B market cap as AI-driven demand for HDDs accelerates.

Ciena received multiple analyst upgrades in March: TD Cowen initiated Buy with $425 price target (added to Top Picks list, March 11); Argus raised target to $370 from $280 (Buy). Both firms cite Ciena as a 'key beneficiary' of AI infrastructure networking demand and its Nubis acquisition for intra-datacenter connectivity. Stock trading around $383 after recent pullback from ~$394 highs.

CF Industries surged ~$6 on March 6 to $117, benefiting from fertilizer/nitrogen price spikes tied to the Iran war energy shock. However, Mizuho downgraded to Underperform from Neutral ($100 target) and BofA raised its target to $103 but maintained Underperform, both arguing the 2026 gains from the oil/fertilizer price surge are overdone and the nitrogen price cycle is temporary.

Nike Q3 FY2026 earnings scheduled for March 31, 2026 — not yet reported. Q2 FY2026 (December 2025) showed a beat: EPS $0.53 vs. $0.37 expected (+43.2%). Q3 consensus EPS estimate is $0.30. Stock under consumer discretionary pressure from Iran-war inflation concerns.

UnitedHealth has fallen sharply from above $600 in late 2024 to ~$292 by late February 2026 — a ~52% decline — driven by Q4 2025 results showing 2026 revenue guidance below consensus ($439B vs. ~$454B expected, a first-ever year-on-year revenue decline), higher medical cost ratios, and ongoing fallout from the Change Healthcare cyberattack. Oppenheimer cut target from $415 to $385, Mizuho cut to $350, Truist to $370. Consensus remains Buy with ~$373 average target. Positioned for potential recovery play.

BWX Technologies upgraded to Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy) on March 20, with consensus EPS estimates rising 10.4% over three months. The nuclear component supplier to the U.S. government has gained ~17.8% over three months, outperforming the S&P 500. FY2025 earnings guided to $4.49/share; the defense/nuclear theme benefits from both the Iran war geopolitical backdrop and long-term nuclear energy revival.

GigaCloud Technology upgraded to Zacks Rank #1 (Strong Buy) on March 20, with consensus EPS estimates for current year rising 17.1% over 60 days. The B2B e-commerce solutions company has gained +20.15% over the past month, significantly outperforming the S&P 500. Carries a Momentum Score of A; forward P/E of 10.3x is well below the industry average of 15.6x.

MongoDB launched Voyage AI embedding models and Model Context Protocol Server (GitHub Copilot/Claude integrations) in public preview in March. BofA names MDB among the first data infra stocks to rebound post-'SaaSpocalypse.' Morgan Stanley reaffirmed Overweight citing accelerating demand and AI use cases. Stock is down ~12% over 3 months but seen as a recovery candidate.
